content.text: Token reduction is the practice of deliberately shrinking the number of tokens in LLM prompts and completions to lower API costs, reduce latency, and stay within context windows — without sacrificing the quality of outputs. It encompasses techniques applied to every part of the prompt: the system instruction, retrieved context, conversation history, and the requested output format.

Tokens are the fundamental unit of LLM pricing and context limits. A token is roughly 3–4 characters in English prose. GPT-4o charges separately for input and output tokens, with output tokens costing 4× more per token than input. Every character in every API call that is not contributing to answer quality is wasted spend.

At small scale, token inefficiency is invisible. A single call that sends 800 unnecessary tokens costs a fraction of a cent. At production scale — 100,000 API calls per day — those 800 tokens per call cost $12 per day or $360 per month on GPT-4o, purely from a bloated system prompt. A team that ships a feature without reviewing token counts is effectively leaving money on the floor every second the system runs.

Token reduction also directly affects latency. LLMs generate tokens one at a time; longer context windows increase time-to-first-token (TTFT) because the model must attend over all input tokens before producing the first output token. In user-facing applications where TTFT matters for perceived responsiveness, reducing prompt length by even 30% can meaningfully improve the user experience.

Context window limits create a hard ceiling: GPT-4o has a 128,000-token context window, but RAG systems that inject large document chunks, long conversation histories, and verbose system prompts can hit this ceiling on real production traffic, causing requests to fail. Token reduction is often the first line of defense before upgrading to more expensive models with larger context windows.

Every token eliminated from the prompt is money saved on every future call. Savings compound: a 60% reduction in system prompt size at $0.0025/1K input tokens on 1 million calls per month saves $1,500/month with zero change to user-visible behavior.
Shorter prompts reduce TTFT. For streaming applications, this means users see the first output character sooner, improving perceived responsiveness.
Compressing system prompts and history frees up budget for longer or more documents in RAG pipelines, enabling better answers on knowledge-intensive queries without hitting context limits.

When token-per-dollar efficiency is maximized for a given use case, the budget freed up can be reallocated to route a subset of complex queries to a more expensive, smarter model, improving answer quality for the cases that need it most.

Removing instructions from a system prompt may cause the model to revert to default behavior, producing outputs that are too long, in the wrong format, or missing required constraints. Every reduction must be validated against a representative eval set.

Summarizing conversation history into a compressed paragraph loses verbatim quotes, specific data points mentioned by the user, and the tone of prior turns. For high-stakes workflows (legal, medical, customer support), this information loss can cause the model to give incorrect answers.

Using a cross-encoder re-ranker to filter retrieved context down from top-10 to top-3 saves input tokens but adds a round-trip to the re-ranking model. The net latency effect depends on the relative speed of the re-ranker vs. the LLM.
A routing layer adds code, latency, and a new failure mode: misclassified queries sent to the wrong model. The routing logic must be tested against real traffic distributions.

LLM API costs are almost entirely token-based: you pay per input token and per output token, with output usually costing 3–4× more. At scale, a poorly optimized prompt is not just a code quality issue — it is a recurring cost on every API call. Token reduction is the practice of intentionally shrinking prompts and responses without degrading the quality of the answers, by removing waste and structuring content more efficiently.

attrs.alt: Token budget breakdown by segment (system prompt, RAG context, history, user message, output) with optimization strategies and estimated savings for each area.

content.text: Never optimize blindly. Count tokens first so you know where to focus, and re-count after each change to verify the reduction. The
library counts tokens locally without an API call, using the same BPE tokenizer that OpenAI models use.

import tiktoken

# cl100k_base is used by gpt-4, gpt-3.5-turbo, and text-embedding-ada-002
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

def count_tokens(text: str) -> int:
    return len(enc.encode(text))

def count_messages(messages: list[dict]) -> int:
    """Count tokens in a chat messages list (per-message overhead included)."""
    total = 3  # every reply is primed with <|start|>assistant<|message|>
    for msg in messages:
        total += 4  # role/name overhead per message
        total += count_tokens(msg.get("content") or "")
    return total


# Before optimizing:
system_before = """You are a helpful, knowledgeable, and friendly AI assistant...
[... 800 tokens of verbose instructions ...]"""
print(f"System prompt: {count_tokens(system_before)} tokens")

# After optimizing:
system_after = """Code reviewer. Rules:
- Flag bugs and security issues.
- Suggest fixes with code snippets.
- Max 3 suggestions per review."""
print(f"Optimized: {count_tokens(system_after)} tokens")
# Prints: System prompt: 800 tokens → Optimized: 38 tokens

System prompts are included in full on every API call. A verbose system prompt that grows by accident to 800 tokens costs the same as sending an extra short paragraph on every request, forever. The most effective reductions come from removing redundant phrasing and converting prose to structured lists.

# Before: 247 tokens of redundant prose
SYSTEM_VERBOSE = """
You are a highly capable, knowledgeable, and professional AI assistant
that specializes in helping software developers. You should always aim to
be helpful and provide accurate, relevant information. When answering
questions, you should always try to be clear, concise, and easy to
understand. You should avoid using overly technical jargon unless it's
necessary, and you should always be respectful and professional in your
responses. If you are unsure about something, you should let the user
know rather than making up information. Always provide code examples
when applicable to help clarify your explanations.
"""

# After: 34 tokens — identical behavior in practice
SYSTEM_COMPACT = """Software dev assistant. Be accurate. Give runnable code examples. Say 'I don't know' if unsure."""

# Verify:
print(count_tokens(SYSTEM_VERBOSE))   # 247
print(count_tokens(SYSTEM_COMPACT))   # 34
: for each sentence in your system prompt, ask "does the model behave differently without this?". Run an eval set with and without the sentence to confirm. Most prompts have 30–50% removable padding.

Retrieved context is often the largest single cost driver because retrieval pipelines tend to fetch more documents than the LLM actually needs. The main strategies are smaller chunks, tighter top-k, and re-ranking to remove low-relevance results before injection.

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()


def retrieve_and_trim(
    query: str,
    vector_store,  # any retrieve(query, top_k) → list[str]
    max_context_tokens: int = 2000,
) -> str:
    # Retrieve more than needed; we'll trim to budget
    docs = vector_store.retrieve(query, top_k=10)

    # Re-rank: keep only docs with enough relevance
    # (In production use Cohere rerank or a cross-encoder)
    reranked = [d for d in docs if d.score >= 0.5]

    # Greedily fill token budget from highest-scoring docs
    context_parts: list[str] = []
    used_tokens = 0
    for doc in reranked:  # already sorted by score desc
        doc_tokens = count_tokens(doc.text)
        if used_tokens + doc_tokens > max_context_tokens:
            break
        context_parts.append(doc.text)
        used_tokens += doc_tokens

    return "\n\n---\n\n".join(context_parts)


def answer_with_rag(question: str, vector_store) -> str:
    context = retrieve_and_trim(question, vector_store)
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": "Answer based on context only. If not found, say so."},
        {"role": "user", "content": f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"},
    ]
    print(f"Prompt tokens: {count_messages(messages)}")
    response = client.chat.completions.create(model="gpt-4o-mini", messages=messages)
    return response.choices[0].message.content

In a long chat, history tokens grow with every turn. A conversation that starts at 200 tokens can reach 8,000 tokens after 20 exchanges. Compression approaches: rolling window (drop oldest turns), summarization (replace N turns with a summary), or a hybrid that preserves the most recent N turns verbatim and summarizes everything older.

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

MAX_HISTORY_TOKENS = 1500
KEEP_RECENT_TURNS = 3


def summarize_history(messages: list[dict]) -> str:
    transcript = "\n".join(
        f"{m['role'].upper()}: {m['content']}" for m in messages
    )
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Summarise this conversation in 3–4 sentences. Preserve key decisions, facts mentioned, and user preferences."},
            {"role": "user", "content": transcript},
        ],
        max_tokens=200,
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content


def compress_history(history: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    if count_messages(history) <= MAX_HISTORY_TOKENS:
        return history  # no compression needed

    # Keep the most recent turns verbatim
    recent = history[-(KEEP_RECENT_TURNS * 2):]
    older = history[:-(KEEP_RECENT_TURNS * 2)]

    if not older:
        return recent  # nothing old enough to compress

    summary_text = summarize_history(older)
    summary_msg = {
        "role": "system",
        "content": f"[Conversation summary so far: {summary_text}]",
    }
    compressed = [summary_msg] + recent
    print(f"History compressed: {count_messages(history)}t → {count_messages(compressed)}t")
    return compressed
Output tokens cost more per token than input tokens. Controlling verbosity directly reduces the most expensive part of the bill.

# 1. Set a realistic max_tokens ceiling
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=messages,
    max_tokens=256,  # don't leave this at default (4096+)
)

# 2. Ask for structured output instead of explanatory prose
# Instead of: "Explain whether the code has bugs."
# Use:
QUERY = """
Analyse for bugs. Respond in JSON only:
{"has_bug": bool, "severity": "low|medium|high", "line": int|null, "fix": str}
"""
# JSON response is 40 tokens; prose version is 200+

# 3. Instruct explicit length limits
SYSTEM = "Answer in one sentence. No preamble."

# 4. Track output tokens in production
def log_usage(response) -> None:
    usage = response.usage
    cost_estimate = (
        usage.prompt_tokens * 0.0025 / 1000     # gpt-4o input rate
        + usage.completion_tokens * 0.01 / 1000  # gpt-4o output rate
    )
    print(f"Input: {usage.prompt_tokens}t  Output: {usage.completion_tokens}t  ~${cost_estimate:.5f}")
Not all tasks need the most capable (and most expensive) model. A common pattern is to use a cheap fast model for simple or high-volume tasks and reserve the expensive model for complex ones.

def route_model(query: str) -> str:
    """Return the appropriate model name based on estimated query complexity."""
    query_tokens = count_tokens(query)

    # Simple heuristic: short queries with no code → cheap model
    has_code = any(kw in query.lower() for kw in ["code", "function", "debug", "implement"])
    is_complex = query_tokens > 200 or has_code

    if is_complex:
        return "gpt-4o"         # $2.50/1M input, $10/1M output
    else:
        return "gpt-4o-mini"    # $0.15/1M input, $0.60/1M output
    # gpt-4o-mini is ~16× cheaper; use it for classification, extraction, short Q&A


def smart_chat(user_message: str) -> str:
    model = route_model(user_message)
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    print(f"Used {model} for {response.usage.total_tokens} tokens")
    return response.choices[0].message.content

# Estimate monthly cost for a given design
PRICING = {
    "gpt-4o": {"input": 2.50, "output": 10.00},  # per 1M tokens
    "gpt-4o-mini": {"input": 0.15, "output": 0.60},
}

def estimate_monthly_cost(
    model: str,
    avg_input_tokens: int,
    avg_output_tokens: int,
    requests_per_month: int,
) -> float:
    p = PRICING[model]
    input_cost = (avg_input_tokens * requests_per_month / 1_000_000) * p["input"]
    output_cost = (avg_output_tokens * requests_per_month / 1_000_000) * p["output"]
    return input_cost + output_cost

# Example: customer support bot, 50k requests/month
before = estimate_monthly_cost("gpt-4o", avg_input_tokens=2000, avg_output_tokens=400, requests_per_month=50_000)
after = estimate_monthly_cost("gpt-4o-mini", avg_input_tokens=600, avg_output_tokens=150, requests_per_month=50_000)

print(f"Before optimization: ${before:.2f}/month")
print(f"After optimization:  ${after:.2f}/month")
print(f"Savings:             ${before - after:.2f}/month")
# Before optimization: $325.00/month
# After optimization:  $9.00/month
# Savings:             $316.00/month
Developers often remove instructions intuitively, without counting tokens before and after. Two paragraphs that "feel long" may only be 80 tokens. Use
to quantify the impact of every change.
Reducing output quality by constraining length too aggressively.
on a query that legitimately requires a 200-token answer produces truncated responses. Set
based on the 95th percentile of output length in your test set, not an arbitrary number.
Forgetting that chat API has per-message overhead.

Every message in a chat history adds 4 tokens of overhead (role + separator tokens). A 20-turn conversation with short messages adds 80 tokens just from overhead. Collapsing multiple user turns into one message reduces this overhead.

# WRONG: not measuring before/after
def compress_system_prompt_wrong(prompt: str) -> str:
    # Remove some words that "look" unnecessary
    return prompt.replace("always ", "").replace("please ", "")
    # No measurement, no validation

# RIGHT: measure, compress, measure again, validate on eval set
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

def compress_system_prompt_right(prompt: str) -> str:
    before = len(enc.encode(prompt))
    # Compress:
    compressed = bullet_point_conversion(prompt)  # your compression logic
    after = len(enc.encode(compressed))
    print(f"Compression: {before}t -> {after}t ({(before-after)/before*100:.0f}% reduction)")
    # Must validate on eval set before deploying
    assert quality_score(compressed) >= quality_threshold, "Quality regression detected"
    return compressed

# WRONG: leaving max_tokens at API default (often 4096)
response = client.chat.completions.create(model="gpt-4o", messages=messages)

# RIGHT: set based on measured output length distribution
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=messages,
    max_tokens=512,  # set from p95 of your eval set
)

Establish a token budget per component (system prompt, RAG context, history, user message, output) and track each component separately in production monitoring. This makes regressions visible immediately.

Write an automated eval suite before optimizing. Run it after every reduction to catch quality regressions. A 30-question representative sample is usually enough to catch gross regressions; a 200-question suite catches subtle ones.

Cache the system prompt and any stable context using prompt caching (Anthropic's cache_control, OpenAI's structured caching) to avoid paying for repeated input tokens. Cached tokens cost 10% of uncached input tokens on Anthropic's API.
When designing RAG pipelines, chunk documents at retrieval time (512–1024 tokens) rather than ingesting entire documents. Smaller chunks give more precise retrieval and a lower per-query token cost.

Prefer structured output (JSON) for any task where the output will be parsed programmatically. A JSON response with 3 fields is 30–50 tokens; a prose paragraph explaining the same information is 150–300 tokens.
Always — first step in any optimization
Stable system prompts, repeated document context
All RAG pipelines fetching more than top-3
Removing safety instructions from the system prompt to save tokens removes safeguards. Never trim safety-critical instructions — only trim redundant filler and formatting padding.

Sending conversation history to a smaller model for summarization may expose sensitive user data to an additional API endpoint. Ensure the summarization model has the same data handling commitments as the primary model, and redact PII before summarization if required by your data agreements.

If the token budget for RAG context is set too low, the system will silently omit relevant chunks. Add logging to alert when the budget is hit, so engineers know that context is being dropped and can investigate whether answer quality is affected.
Token reduction is the highest-ROI cost optimization for LLM applications — the savings apply to every future API call with zero hardware or infrastructure changes.
Always measure with tiktoken before and after each change; intuitions about prompt length are often wrong.
The four optimization levers are: system prompt compression, RAG context trimming, conversation history compression, and output verbosity control.
Output tokens cost 3–4× more than input tokens; controlling output verbosity (via explicit length instructions and structured output) is especially high leverage.
Model routing — sending simple queries to cheap models and complex ones to expensive models — can achieve the same quality profile at 80–95% lower cost for mixed workloads.
Prompt caching (where available) eliminates cost on stable system prompts and repeated context at near-zero implementation cost.
Every reduction must be validated on a representative eval set — removing tokens without testing is a quality regression waiting to happen.
Replace prose instructions with bullet points and concrete examples.
Cap RAG context to a fixed token budget; prefer top-3 re-ranked chunks over top-10 raw results.
Compress conversation history when it exceeds a token threshold, keeping the N most recent turns verbatim.
explicitly; never leave it at the API default.
Ask for JSON or structured answers when a prose explanation is not needed.
Route simple, short queries to a smaller model; reserve large models for complex reasoning.
per request to catch regressions early.
